stream: Competitive landscape research: AI product that ingests a company's full footprint (website, news, social, brand) and auto-generates ICP-tuned social posts, with a focus on images + carousels.
summary: The market splits into four camps that each own one piece of the wedge but none own all three: (1) paid-ad creative engines (AdCreative.ai) with strong competitor-intel and scoring but generic, templated output; (2) organic social generators (Predis.ai, Ocoya, SocialPost.ai) that do carousels + brand kits but produce mid-tier visual quality with shallow ICP logic; (3) carousel specialists (PostNitro, ContentDrips, aiCarousels, Draft AI, Taplio) with the best layout discipline but no company-intelligence ingestion; and (4) writing/brand suites (Jasper, Copy.ai, Typeface) where visuals are an afterthought. The "ingest your whole company automatically" play is barely staked out — only SocialPost.ai explicitly does website-to-content auto-extraction, and even it is light on carousel craft and ICP segmentation. Carousel quality (per-slide art direction, text rendering, design hierarchy) and ICP-specific targeting are the two most universally weak axes; competitor/reference visual research exists only in fragmented design-inspo tools (Inspo AI, Savee) that don't generate posts. The sharpest open wedge is a product that fuses auto-ingested company intelligence + true ICP segmentation + carousel-grade design craft + competitor visual reference research into one loop. BR market is thin and shallow (GalilAI, Postig, PostNitro-PT), leaving room for an ICP-aware, Portuguese-native entrant.

findings.claim: AdCreative.ai is the strongest on competitor intel + scoring but its output is widely criticized as generic/templated.
findings.detail: Tiers run ~$39/mo Starter to $249/mo Professional (some sources cite $29 Startup / $99 Ultimate / $149 Scale-Up), 40% annual discount. Signature features: Creative Scoring AI (340 data points, claimed 90% accuracy), Competitor Insights AI (see competitors' best-performing creatives across platforms), trained on $35B+ ad spend. Does image-to-video. Biggest weakness: reviewers report output is templated/interchangeable with competitor ads, rigid templates, limited layout control ('production tool, not creative partner'), plus documented billing/trial-charge complaints and slow support. It is built for PAID ads at volume, not carousel craft or organic ICP storytelling.

findings.claim: Predis.ai is the leading dedicated organic-social generator and the closest 'carousel + brand' incumbent, but visual sophistication and ICP targeting are shallow.
findings.detail: Generates posts, carousels, reels, ads from a text prompt — handles copy + visual design + captions + hashtags, applies a brand setup (logo, colors, tone). Pricing ~$32/mo Lite (60 posts) / ~$59 Premium / $249 Agency; credit math ~26-43 carousels/mo on a 1,300-credit Core plan. Has AI competitor analysis and content scoring + best-time-to-post. Weaknesses: comparison sources note NO detail/control over image quality or resolution; targeting is industry-vertical (ecommerce/agency/influencer) rather than granular ICP segments; does not clearly auto-ingest a website or brand assets — user still feeds prompts/brand setup manually.

findings.claim: SocialPost.ai is the most direct threat to the 'ingest everything about the company' thesis — it is explicitly website-to-content.
findings.detail: Analyzes a business's website, content, brand assets, blogs, tone, services, and products to auto-generate ready-to-publish posts that reflect the brand visually and contextually, removing the need to manually define brand colors/voice/direction. This is precisely the auto-ingestion wedge. Gaps: public sources are thin on carousel-specific craft, ICP segmentation depth, and pricing; positioning is 'first AI engine designed for social' / website-to-posts rather than per-ICP, per-slide carousel design. The auto-ingestion idea is no longer novel, so an entrant must out-execute on ICP + carousel quality, not just on ingestion.

findings.claim: Carousel specialists (PostNitro, ContentDrips, aiCarousels, Draft AI) own the best layout discipline and brand-kit consistency but do NOT ingest company intelligence.
findings.detail: PostNitro: 100+ templates, Brand Kit (exact brand colors/fonts/logo per slide), API, multi-platform (LinkedIn/IG/TikTok/X/Threads); free plan + paid from $10/mo. ContentDrips: strong AI generation quality + brand-kit consistency + bulk CSV; Free ($0, 50 credits) / Starter $15 (or $12 annual) / Pro $26 (or $21 annual, unlimited). Draft AI: voice-memo -> carousel, learns your aesthetic, Brand Kit, free + Pro $15/mo. aiCarousels: fastest specialized LinkedIn/IG carousel tool. Common weakness across all: they start from a user prompt/voice/CSV — none crawl a company's website, news, or socials to decide WHAT to post or WHO to target. Pure execution layer, no intelligence layer.

findings.claim: Taplio and Postwise own the LinkedIn/X carousel niche but are personal-brand tools, not company-intelligence engines.
findings.detail: Taplio: LinkedIn personal branding — AI Carousel Generator (auto-carousels from your posts), AI post generator, image generation, scheduling, analytics, lead export. Pricing $39/mo Starter (NO AI credits — scheduling/analytics only), Growth $69, Pro $199; the AI features it markets require the mid/top tiers. Postwise: X/Twitter focused, from $49/mo, generates ideas from URLs/trends; a March 2026 update added a carousel optimizer + A/B hook testing. Weakness: single-platform, individual-creator framing, no automatic ingestion of a company's full footprint or ICP modeling.

findings.claim: Canva Magic Studio is the visual-quality and bulk-production benchmark, but it has no autonomous strategy/ICP/ingestion layer — it is a manual design surface.
findings.detail: Magic Design generates 8-12 full templates from one prompt; Brand Kit auto-applies colors/fonts/logos to AI output; Bulk Create makes hundreds of variants from a CSV; Magic Switch converts one design into a carousel or 100+ languages. Full suite (incl. Dream Lab 500 images/mo) at Canva Pro $14.99/mo. Weakness for this thesis: it does not decide what to post, does not ingest company news/socials/competitors, and does no ICP targeting — a human must drive every decision. It is the production engine a smarter strategy layer would ideally sit on top of or compete against on craft.

findings.claim: Jasper, Copy.ai, and Typeface are brand/writing-first suites where carousels and visuals are weak or absent.
findings.detail: Jasper: $39 Creator / $59 Pro / Business custom; brand voice + Jasper IQ (learns brand guides), Jasper Art images, instant campaigns; image/carousel craft is secondary to copy. Copy.ai: pivoted to a B2B GTM platform (Workflows/agents), Starter $49 / Advanced $249 / Enterprise; Brand Voice analyzes a company's website for tone — but it is now revenue-ops, not visual social. Typeface: enterprise-only, custom pricing (est. $49-99/user/mo up to high six/seven figures), 'Blend' trains on brand assets for multimodal text+image+video, Brand Hub; weeks-long non-self-serve onboarding, SOC2/SSO. Weakness: none of the three is a fast, self-serve, carousel-grade, ICP-tuned social engine — Jasper/Copy.ai under-deliver on visuals, Typeface is too heavy/expensive for SMB-mid.

findings.claim: Management suites (Hootsuite OwlyWriter, Buffer AI, FeedHive, Vista Social, Highperformr, Flick) bolt AI captions onto scheduling — AI generation is a feature, not the product.
findings.detail: Hootsuite OwlyWriter: captions/ideas/carousels, in Professional $99/mo, learns historic post tone. Buffer AI: simple/cheap caption helper. FeedHive: $19-299/mo, AI writing + content recycling + performance prediction on higher tiers. Vista Social: $64-$304/mo + Enterprise, AI captions + Competitive Intelligence monitoring. Highperformr: AI content + intent-signal intelligence + CRM enrichment (B2B intent angle, not carousel craft). Flick: 'Iris' AI for captions/visuals/hashtags + scheduler; ~$132-$816/yr. Common weakness: visuals/carousels are thin, no deep company ingestion, ICP is coarse — these win on distribution/scheduling, lose on creative generation quality.

findings.claim: Submagic and Brandwell are adjacent, not direct competitors — they solve different jobs.
findings.detail: Submagic is short-form VIDEO editing (auto-captions 99% accuracy, silence removal, B-roll, clip detection); from $12/member/mo (Pro $23, Business+API $41). It is not an image/carousel post generator and does not do company ingestion or ICP — relevant only as a video-side complement. Brandwell (formerly Content at Scale) is long-form SEO/blog writing + a 'who's researching your competitors' intent layer; Essentials $249/mo, Agency $499/mo, API $4/post. It is content-marketing/SEO, not visual social — overlaps only on the 'company intelligence' framing, not on carousels.

findings.claim: Competitor/reference VISUAL research is a separate, unconnected tool category — nobody fuses it into post generation.
findings.detail: Inspo AI offers natural-language design search, collaborative moodboards with palettes/font pairs, AI brand-compliance auditing, and competitor website scanning — but it stops at inspiration, it does not generate finished ICP-targeted carousels. Savee ('More Like This' = Spotify Radio for imagery) and Milanote are pure moodboard/reference tools for designers. AdCreative's Competitor Insights shows competitor AD creatives but feeds a generic ad engine, not bespoke carousel design. The gap: no product crawls competitors'/category-leaders' actual visual social output, distills the design language, and uses it to art-direct your own ICP-tuned carousels.

findings.claim: The Brazil market is shallow: a few thin carousel generators, no ICP-aware or auto-ingestion player.
findings.detail: GalilAI (galilai.com.br): generates personalized IG posts (designs/captions/hashtags) from brand identity — but lightweight, no documented website-ingestion or ICP segmentation. Postig (postig.com.br): 'full carousel for your niche in 1 minute,' 3 free carousels — niche-template based, shallow. PostNitro offers a full PT-BR experience but is a global template tool, not BR-specific or ICP-aware. International tools (Simplified, Dreamina/CapCut, aiCarousels) have PT-BR pages but no BR-market depth. No BR player combines auto company-ingestion + ICP targeting + carousel craft — a clear opening for a Portuguese-native entrant.

findings.claim: Two axes are weak across the ENTIRE field: genuine ICP targeting and per-slide carousel design craft.
findings.detail: On ICP: tools at best let you 'select a target audience' (AdCreative) or segment by industry vertical (Predis) — none model a specific ICP (pains, vocabulary, objections, buying triggers) and re-art-direct each carousel slide to that profile. On carousel craft: incumbents reuse uniform templates (AdCreative 'templated/interchangeable'; Predis 'no detail on visual quality'); the field still struggles with differentiated per-slide art direction, text-in-image rendering, and design hierarchy beyond a single fixed template applied to every slide. This matches Luigi's own field note (B2B carousel anatomy must differ per slide; 6 same-structure slides = '6 capas em loop') — incumbents systematically violate this.

recommendations: WEDGE 1 — Company-intelligence-to-ICP graph as the engine, not just brand kit. Auto-crawl website + news + the company's own social + 2-3 named competitors, then synthesize an explicit ICP object (pains, vocabulary, objections, buying triggers, proof points) that drives WHAT to post and to WHOM. Incumbents stop at 'brand colors/voice' (SocialPost.ai) or 'pick an audience' (AdCreative); none expose a real, editable ICP profile that re-targets every post. Make the ICP the visible, steerable artifact.

WEDGE 2 — Carousel-grade per-slide art direction as the core craft moat. Treat a carousel as a designed narrative with DIFFERENT anatomy per slide (cover that opens the theme, middles that carry one stat/quote/list each, a closing CTA) rather than one template stamped N times. Solve text-in-image rendering and design hierarchy that AdCreative/Predis are criticized for. This is the single most under-served axis and the hardest to copy — own it as the headline differentiator.

WEDGE 3 — Competitor/reference VISUAL research fused INTO generation. Crawl the actual visual social output of category leaders + named competitors, distill their design language (palette, type, layout patterns, hook styles), and use it to art-direct your ICP-tuned carousels — then show the reference alongside the output for trust. Today this lives in disconnected inspo tools (Inspo AI, Savee) OR as competitor-AD galleries (AdCreative); nobody closes the loop from competitor visual research -> bespoke on-brand, on-ICP carousel.

Beachhead on BR + Portuguese-native, ICP-aware content. The BR field (GalilAI, Postig) is shallow and template-driven with no ICP or auto-ingestion; a Portuguese-first product that ingests a BR company's site/socials and produces ICP-tuned carousels has a defensible local opening before global players localize depth.

Position against the incumbent gap explicitly in messaging: 'Not another template stamper.' AdCreative's loudest complaint is generic/templated output and billing opacity; Predis's is no visual-quality control. Lead with design craft + transparent pricing + an editable ICP, the three things the category leaders are weakest on.

Consider sitting on or rivaling Canva-grade rendering rather than competing on raw image gen. Canva owns production quality and bulk; the entrant's edge is the intelligence + ICP + competitor-research layer that decides and art-directs — pair best-in-class rendering with a strategy brain the incumbents lack.
risks: Auto-ingestion is no longer novel: SocialPost.ai already markets website-to-content, and Copy.ai/Jasper/Typeface analyze a company's site for brand voice. Ingestion alone is not a moat — the defensibility must come from ICP modeling + carousel craft + competitor visual research, which are harder to build but also harder to copy.

Canva (Magic Studio at $14.99/mo) and Predis ($32/mo) anchor pricing low for 'good enough' output; an entrant charging a premium must prove visibly superior carousel quality or ICP precision or it will be compared on price and lose.

Carousel design craft (per-slide art direction, reliable text-in-image, hierarchy) is genuinely hard — it is exactly where every incumbent is weak BECAUSE it is hard. Underestimating the engineering/design effort here turns the headline differentiator into the same templated output everyone ships.

Crawling competitors' visual social output and reusing distilled design language raises IP/ToS and platform-scraping risk (Instagram/LinkedIn API limits); the reference-research feature needs a compliant data-sourcing strategy.

Pulling 'EVERYTHING about a company' (news, social, brand) implies brittle scraping pipelines and stale/incorrect data; bad ingestion produces off-brand or factually wrong posts, which is reputationally fatal for a content tool. Needs human-in-the-loop verification of the ICP/brand facts before generation.

Market is crowded and feature-converging: every suite is bolting on AI captions + carousels + brand kit + competitor view. A thin differentiator gets copied within a release cycle; the wedge must be a system (ingestion -> ICP -> competitor visual research -> per-slide craft as one loop), not a single feature.

stream: Company Intelligence Profile Extraction — Technical Methods & Stack
summary: For automated company intelligence extraction feeding a content engine, the strongest 2026 stack pairs Firecrawl (website crawl + the native `branding` format that returns logo/colors/fonts/spacing in one call) with Apify pay-per-result actors for social (Instagram, LinkedIn, TikTok) at roughly $1-2 per 1,000 results, and a free/cheap news layer (GDELT DOC 2.0 + Google News RSS for breadth at $0, optionally NewsData.io for commercial structured signals). Brand-kit extraction can be off-loaded to a dedicated API (Firecrawl branding format or Brandprint) or self-hosted with node-vibrant/ColorThief for colors plus CSS/@font-face parsing for fonts. Voice/tone/positioning synthesis is best done by feeding the scraped corpus (about/products/blog/social captions) into an LLM that produces a structured one-page "brand voice brief" (personality traits, vocabulary rules, banned phrases, formatting signals, sentiment arc). The whole thing maps cleanly onto a provider-agnostic adapter interface (CrawlSource, SocialSource, NewsSource, BrandSource, VoiceSynthesizer) so any single provider can be swapped without touching the engine.

findings.claim: Firecrawl is the strongest single website-extraction layer: crawl + map + AI extract + native branding format
findings.detail: Three core verbs: Search, Scrape (clean LLM-ready markdown by default; also raw HTML, screenshots, metadata, JSON-via-schema), Interact (click/navigate for SPAs). /map returns every URL on a site (use to find /about, /products, /pricing, /blog deterministically). /crawl follows links with depth/page-limit/path filters. /extract pulls structured JSON via schema across one page or whole site. JS rendering is automatic, so SPAs (Wix, Framer, Next.js) work. Pricing: 1 credit = 1 page standard; AI extraction = 5 credits/request. Free 1,000 credits/mo; Hobby $16/mo (3,000 credits ~ 600 AI extractions); Growth $333/mo (500k). The 5x extract multiplier is the main hidden cost.

findings.claim: Firecrawl's `branding` format returns a full brand kit in one scrape call
findings.detail: Add `formats=['branding']` to scrape(). Returns logo URL (v2 handles background-image/complex HTML edge cases), hex color palette, font families, spacing scale, and UI component styles — structured and ready for an on-brand content generator. Works with modern site builders. Pricing not separately documented (priced as a scrape). This is the cheapest path to a brand kit if you are already crawling the site with Firecrawl.

findings.claim: Dedicated brand-extraction APIs exist if you want one call decoupled from crawling
findings.detail: Brandprint.io advertises colors+fonts+logos from any URL with 95%+ accuracy in <400ms, full kit per call (page is 403 to bots; verify pricing directly). Ad Legends and Brand Extractor (genysysengine) are URL-in/kit-out web tools. Chrome extensions (Brand Kit Extractor, Peek, Brand Element Extractor) exist for manual one-offs but are not API-automatable. For a pipeline, prefer Firecrawl branding or Brandprint over extensions.

findings.claim: Self-hosted brand-kit extraction is cheap and removes a vendor dependency
findings.detail: COLORS: node-vibrant (semantic swatches Vibrant/Muted/DarkVibrant/LightMuted, Android Palette-style, auto-downsamples for consistent runtime) or ColorThief (MMCQ quantization, getPaletteSync/getSwatches, faster for high throughput). Run on a hero screenshot or scraped imagery. LOGO: pull from og:image / <link rel=icon> / largest header SVG. FONTS: parse computed font-family + @font-face declarations + Google Fonts/Adobe Typekit <link> tags + .woff/.woff2 network requests; Wappalyzer detects the font service. Apify also has automation-lab/font-detector actor for hosted font ID.

findings.claim: Apify is the practical social-scraping layer; pay-per-result actors are cheap and reliable for IG/LinkedIn/TikTok
findings.detail: INSTAGRAM: official apify/instagram-scraper extracts posts (captions, hashtags, media, timestamps), engagement (likes/comments/comment text), profiles (followers, bio, verification) at from $1.50/1k results; apify/instagram-post-scraper and reel-scraper at $1.00/1k; all six first-party apify/* IG actors show 100% run success in 2026. LINKEDIN: harvestapi/linkedin-company-posts — post content, author, engagement (likes/comments/shares + reaction breakdown), reposts, media; NO cookies/login; ~$2/1k posts; 4.9/5, ~8h issue response. TikTok: clockworks/tiktok-profile-scraper or apidojo/tiktok-profile-scraper — bio, followers, full video history + engagement at $0.30/1k posts, ~98% success, no proxy. Reliability caveat: IG ships anti-bot changes often — run a 10-item pilot before any backfill and keep concurrency low.

findings.claim: Apify billing is hybrid: pay-per-result actor price PLUS platform compute, with prepaid monthly credits
findings.detail: Free $0/mo includes $5 store credit, $0.2/compute unit; Starter $29/mo ($29 credit, $0.16/CU); Scale $199/mo ($199 credit, $0.16/CU); Business $999/mo ($0.13/CU). Prepaid credits cover usage; overage bills next invoice; unused credits do NOT roll over. For most company-profile use the per-result fee dominates and a single company (a few hundred posts across 3 networks) costs well under $1-2, so the Free or Starter tier is sufficient.

findings.claim: News/PR signals: GDELT + Google News RSS give zero-cost breadth; paid APIs add structure and full text
findings.detail: FREE: GDELT DOC 2.0 API — full-text search of all monitored coverage (last 24h for FTS, broader for DOC), Boolean + near5 proximity + sourcelang + domain + GKG theme filters, sorted by relevance/date/sentiment, $0 (Python client: gdeltdoc on PyPI). Google News RSS — https://news.google.com/rss/search?q="Company Name" with OR/quotes/-exclude operators, no API key, reliable for years but undocumented/can change. PAID: NewsData.io free tier is commercial-OK (200 credits/day, 10 articles/credit; Basic $199.99/mo for 20k credits, AI enrichment, 97k+ sources); Mediastack cheaper entry ($24.99/mo) but low volume; NewsAPI.org free is dev-only (no commercial, 100 req/day, no full text). NewsAPI.ai/Event Registry adds NLP entity/event extraction. For sales-style trigger events, Autobound-style B2B feeds pre-associate news to companies.

findings.claim: Voice/tone/positioning is an LLM synthesis step over the scraped corpus, output as a structured brief
findings.detail: Feed the scraped about/products/blog copy + top social captions into an LLM and ask for a one-page brand voice brief: personality traits, tone guidelines by scenario, vocabulary rules, banned phrases/hedge words, reading level, formatting signals (headings, bullets, CTA placement, em-dash vs parentheses), recurring topics/entities (e.g. fintech -> risk/regulation/trust), and the typical sentiment arc. Best practice: derive voice from best-PERFORMING published content (high-engagement posts), not stated guidelines; store the brief as a reusable system prompt that the content engine references per channel (LinkedIn vs IG vs blog adapt length/tone).

recommendations: RECOMMENDED STACK (provider-agnostic adapters): (1) CrawlSource -> Firecrawl /map to discover /about /products /pricing /blog, then /scrape (markdown) on those, /extract (JSON schema) for facts like products/offices/team. (2) BrandSource -> Firecrawl `branding` format as primary (already crawling), with a self-hosted node-vibrant + @font-face/Wappalyzer fallback adapter, and Brandprint as a swap-in if you want it decoupled. (3) SocialSource -> Apify call-actor: apify/instagram-scraper, harvestapi/linkedin-company-posts, clockworks/tiktok-profile-scraper. (4) NewsSource -> GDELT DOC 2.0 + Google News RSS for free breadth, NewsData.io as the paid/commercial structured adapter. (5) VoiceSynthesizer -> LLM step producing the structured brand-voice brief consumed by the content engine.

Define a single normalized CompanyProfile schema (identity, products[], brandKit{logo,colors[],fonts[],spacing}, social{platform,posts[],engagement}, news[], voiceBrief) so every adapter maps INTO it and the content engine never depends on a raw provider response. Each adapter = one interface (fetch -> normalize).

Cost control: Firecrawl AI extract is 5 credits/req — prefer plain scrape+markdown and run your own LLM extraction where volume is high; reserve /extract for hard structured fields. On Apify, gate every actor run with a 10-item pilot before full backfill to absorb IG/LinkedIn anti-bot drift.

Per-company cost is small: a typical full profile (site crawl ~30-50 pages, a few hundred social posts across 3 networks, news from free sources) lands around $0.50-$2 in Apify + a handful of Firecrawl credits, so start on Firecrawl Hobby ($16) + Apify Free/Starter and scale tiers only when running many companies.
Cache and content-hash everything (raw HTML, screenshots, datasets) so re-runs of the same company are cheap and brand-kit/voice synthesis only re-fires when source content actually changed.

Voice synthesis should privilege high-engagement social posts (real voice) over website boilerplate (aspirational voice); store the brief as a versioned system prompt and regenerate it on a schedule or when new posts cross an engagement threshold.
risks: Social-platform anti-bot volatility: Instagram/LinkedIn/TikTok change rendering and defenses frequently; even 100%-success Apify actors can break overnight. Mitigate with pilots, low concurrency, the actor Issues tab, and at least one alternate actor per network in the adapter config.

Legal/ToS exposure: scraping LinkedIn/Instagram/TikTok and storing personal/engagement data has ToS and privacy (GDPR/LGPD) implications. Restrict to public company pages, document lawful basis, and avoid storing personal data of commenters beyond what the content engine needs.

Free news sources are undocumented/unstable: Google News RSS URL formats are unofficial and GDELT FTS only covers last 24h — for production-grade, commercial, full-text news, budget for a paid adapter (NewsData.io / NewsAPI.ai) rather than relying solely on free feeds.

Brand-kit accuracy varies by site: logo/color/font auto-detection misfires on heavily image-driven or canvas/WebGL sites; always keep a screenshot + manual-override path and treat the extracted kit as a draft, not ground truth.
Firecrawl extract 5x credit multiplier silently shrinks your credit pool 5x — easy to blow a plan if the engine over-uses AI extract; enforce a budget/quota in the CrawlSource adapter.
Vendor lock-in if adapters leak provider shapes into the content engine; enforce the normalized CompanyProfile boundary in code review.

stream: Programmatic generation of scroll-stopping images and legible-text carousels (1080x1350) for B2B
summary: For B2B carousels where every slide must be legible at thumbnail scale, the proven pattern wins: AI generates the texture/hero visual, a deterministic layer renders the text. The research strongly VALIDATES this thesis. Generative diffusion models cannot reliably produce correct, brand-precise typography (even the best ones top out around 90-95% accuracy on SHORT strings and degrade fast with paragraphs, exact fonts, kerning, and hex-exact brand colors), so embedding text in the generation is a quality and consistency liability for multi-slide decks. Among generators, Ideogram 3.0, Recraft V3/V4, FLUX.2, Imagen 4, and Seedream 4.x render text far better than Midjourney (~30%) or older models, but none guarantee the pixel-perfect, repeatable, on-brand text a deterministic renderer gives for free. The recommended hybrid stack pairs a generation API (fal.ai as the aggregator for FLUX/Recraft/Ideogram/Seedream) for backgrounds/heroes with an HTML/CSS->PNG renderer (Playwright for full CSS fidelity, or Satori+resvg for serverless/edge) as the text layer — matching the already-proven the proven HTML->PNG pipeline. Templated SaaS (Templated.io, Placid, Bannerbear) is the fastest path if you want a hosted template API instead of self-hosting Playwright; Canva Connect API is powerful for brand templates but gated behind Enterprise.

findings.claim: Diffusion image models cannot reliably render correct text; the universal best practice is to separate text from image generation
findings.detail: Diffusion generators process prompts as visual patterns, not linguistic structures, so they depict text-like shapes rather than spell reliably. The consensus recommendation is to generate text-free imagery, then overlay typography deterministically (Figma/Photoshop/HTML-CSS) to control font, kerning, color contrast, and placement. Mitigations when forced to embed: quote the exact string, keep it SHORT, uppercase, no punctuation/mixed-case. This directly validates the AI-texture + deterministic-text thesis.

findings.claim: Ideogram 3.0 is the best-in-class for in-image text but still only ~90-95% accurate on SHORT strings
findings.detail: Ideogram 3.0 (March 2025, ex-Google team) renders text at roughly 90-95% accuracy on short text vs Midjourney v7 at ~30%. Strong for posters/logos/marketing layouts. API followed weeks after model launch. Pricing ~$0.06/image official; on aggregators by speed tier: Turbo ~$0.03-0.0375, Default ~$0.075, Quality ~$0.09-0.1125. Even at best-in-class, it is NOT guaranteed legible for paragraphs or brand-exact fonts — fine for hero accents, not for the carousel body copy.

findings.claim: FLUX.2 (fal.ai) improved typography and supports hex-exact brand colors; cheap per-megapixel billing
findings.detail: FLUX.2 [pro] $0.03 first megapixel + $0.015/extra MP (1024x1024 ~= $0.03, 1920x1080 ~= $0.045). FLUX.2 [dev] $0.012/MP. FLUX.2 Turbo: 1024x1024 in 6.6s at ~$0.008. Generates crisper readable text for mockups/infographics and lets you specify brand colors via exact hex codes. Best general-purpose photoreal background/hero engine for the hybrid stack, but text in-gen is still not deterministic.

findings.claim: Recraft V3/V4 leads vector/logo/clean-graphic work and handles longer text passages; cheapest legit text-capable raster
findings.detail: Recraft V3 ranked #1 on text-to-image arena (ELO 1172, 72% win rate). Excels at flat vector + icon-style illustration and can render LONGER text passages than most. API pricing fixed: $0.04 raster image, $0.08 vector image; Recraft 20B cheaper at $0.022 raster / $0.044 vector. Best choice when you want vector-style B2B graphics or SVG output, but still not a substitute for a deterministic text renderer for guaranteed brand typography.

findings.claim: Google Imagen 4 renders crisp legible text but with strict short-string limits; transparent per-image pricing
findings.detail: Imagen 4 produces legible typography even at small sizes, but Google explicitly recommends <25 characters per phrase and 2-3 phrases max per image — i.e., it confirms the model is unreliable beyond short text. Pricing: Imagen 4 Fast $0.02, Imagen 4 $0.04, Imagen 4 Ultra $0.06 per output image. Available via Gemini API, Google AI Studio, Vertex AI. Good clean background engine with occasional short accent text.

findings.claim: Seedream 4.x has the strongest current in-image text + 4K, but Midjourney has NO usable official API
findings.detail: Seedream 4.5 (ByteDance) is cited as having largely solved text rendering at 4K and is 'indispensable' for text-heavy visuals; on fal Seedream V4 is ~$0.03/image. Conversely, as of early 2026 Midjourney has NO broadly available official public API/REST/SDK/webhook — only Discord/web UI and invitation-only Enterprise. Unofficial Midjourney APIs risk account bans. CONCLUSION: Midjourney is unsuitable as a programmatic node; Seedream/FLUX/Recraft/Ideogram via fal are the programmable options.

findings.claim: HTML/CSS->PNG is the guaranteed-legible text layer; two implementations with a clear trade-off
findings.detail: Playwright/Puppeteer: render an HTML/CSS template at exact 1080x1350 with deviceScaleFactor for retina-sharp text, page.setContent()+page.screenshot(); FULL CSS support (grid, fonts, effects) but each browser instance eats 100-300MB RAM (~4-6 concurrent on a 2GB box). Satori (Vercel) + resvg-js: JSX/HTML+CSS -> SVG -> PNG, the @vercel/og engine, runs serverless/edge with no browser, BUT only flexbox + a CSS subset (no display:grid). Both deliver pixel-perfect, repeatable, brand-exact typography — exactly what the carousel body needs and what AI cannot guarantee.

findings.claim: Templated SaaS APIs are the buy-vs-build alternative to self-hosted Playwright; costs and positioning differ
findings.detail: Templated.io: from $29/mo for 1,000 renders, supports Canva template import, best value + editor UX. Placid: from $15 for 500 credits (image+video), Pro $39/mo for 2,500; strong no-code integrations (Webflow/WordPress/Airtable). Bannerbear: from $49/mo for 1,000 image/video credits, premium/reliable positioning. All let you design a template once and autofill via API — same deterministic-text guarantee as HTML->PNG without running infra.

findings.claim: Canva Connect API (Brand Templates + Autofill + Export) is powerful but gated behind Canva Enterprise
findings.detail: Brand Template and Autofill APIs require the integration to act on behalf of a user in a Canva ENTERPRISE org (custom-quote pricing only). Workflow: GET dataset to discover fields -> upload image assets (async, poll) -> POST autofill job (text + image fields; no video/external URLs) -> poll job -> export via Create design export job. All async/polling. Note: brand template IDs migrated to a new format Sept 2025 (old IDs accepted 6 months). Great if already on Canva Enterprise; otherwise the Enterprise gate makes Templated/Placid/self-host more practical.

findings.claim: fal.ai is the right aggregation layer: one API for FLUX/Recraft/Ideogram/Seedream/Imagen/Nano Banana with per-MP billing
findings.detail: fal.ai exposes FLUX family, Seedream V4 ($0.03/img), Nano Banana ($0.0398/MP), Qwen ($0.02/MP), Flux Kontext Pro ($0.04/img) and more under one billing/API surface with pay-per-use per-megapixel/per-image pricing. This lets the hybrid stack swap the background/hero engine per use case (photoreal=FLUX.2, vector=Recraft, 4K/text-accent=Seedream/Ideogram) without integrating each vendor separately.

recommendations: ADOPT THE HYBRID AS DEFAULT: AI generates the background/texture/hero ONLY (text-free prompts), and a deterministic renderer composites ALL text. This is validated by the research and matches the proven the proven HTML->PNG pipeline — keep it.

TEXT LAYER (the guaranteed-legible part): Standardize on an HTML/CSS template rendered at exactly 1080x1350 with deviceScaleFactor=2 (effective 2160x2700) for retina-sharp type. Use Playwright if you need full CSS (grid, complex effects, web fonts) — your existing the operator brand pipeline likely already does this. Use Satori+resvg only if you move to serverless/edge and can live with flexbox-only layout.

BACKGROUND/HERO ENGINE: Route through fal.ai as the single aggregator. Default to FLUX.2 [dev/Turbo] for photoreal/abstract B2B textures (cheap, ~$0.008-0.012/MP, hex brand colors). Use Recraft V3 ($0.04 raster / $0.08 vector) when you want clean vector/icon/flat graphic backgrounds. Reserve Ideogram 3.0 or Seedream 4.x only for the rare case you want a SHORT decorative text accent baked into the hero — never for body copy.
DO NOT rely on Midjourney programmatically: there is no usable official API and unofficial wrappers risk bans. Drop it from any automated stack.

NEVER embed carousel body copy, stats, or CTAs in the AI generation — even best-in-class models cap at ~90-95% on short strings and fail on paragraphs, exact fonts, kerning, and brand hex. Keep these 100% in the deterministic layer for legibility + brand consistency across all 6-10 slides.

BUY-VS-BUILD for the template layer: if you want to avoid running Playwright infra, use Templated.io ($29/mo, 1k renders, Canva import) or Placid ($15-39/mo, strong no-code integrations). Choose Canva Connect API ONLY if you are already on Canva Enterprise (it is gated and custom-quote). For Luigi's existing self-hosted pipeline, staying on HTML->PNG is cheaper and more controllable than any SaaS.

RECOMMENDED HYBRID STACK FOR B2B CAROUSELS: (1) Content/copy -> structured JSON per slide; (2) fal.ai FLUX.2 generates per-slide text-free background/texture (vary anatomy: cover hero vs. subtle texture on body slides, per the carousel-anatomy lesson in memory); (3) HTML/CSS template (Playwright at 2x scale) composites brand fonts, stats, CTAs over the AI texture -> exports 1080x1350 PNG per slide; (4) optional QA pass (screenshot diff / virality check). This gives scroll-stopping visuals + 100% legible, on-brand text, deterministically and at low per-image cost.
risks: RAM/concurrency ceiling with self-hosted Playwright: 100-300MB per browser instance, ~4-6 concurrent on a 2GB box. Batch carousel renders or use a render queue; consider Satori+resvg for high-volume serverless to avoid the browser entirely (at the cost of grid/CSS support).

Satori limitation: only flexbox + a CSS subset (no display:grid, limited advanced CSS). A complex bento/editorial carousel layout may not render correctly — validate the template in Satori before committing, or stay on Playwright.

Font loading is the #1 silent failure in HTML->PNG: if a custom/brand font isn't embedded/awaited before screenshot, you get a fallback font and broken brand. Embed fonts (base64/local) and await font load explicitly in the renderer.

Generative model text is a trap for non-experts: stakeholders may ask to 'just let the AI write the headline.' Even Ideogram/Seedream degrade on longer or brand-specific text; resist embedding load-bearing copy in the generation.

Vendor/access volatility: Midjourney has no official API; Canva Autofill requires Enterprise; aggregator model availability and pricing on fal.ai shift frequently. Abstract the generation node behind one interface so models can be swapped without rewiring the pipeline.

Cost can creep at scale if defaulting to premium tiers (Ideogram Quality ~$0.09-0.1125/img, FLUX.2 pro per-MP) for backgrounds that a $0.008-0.02 Turbo/Fast tier would serve. Match tier to need; backgrounds rarely justify the top quality tier since text legibility comes from the deterministic layer, not the AI.

stream: ICP derivation, content pillars, and high-converting carousel frameworks for an LLM content pipeline
summary: Research synthesizes four linked frameworks into one pipeline. (1) ICP/persona derivation from public company data uses a 5-dimension model — firmographics, technographics, behavioral signals, organizational readiness, negative indicators — layered with JTBD and B2B buying-committee role mapping (champion, economic buyer, technical evaluator, end user, blocker). (2) Content pillars are 3 (not topic-categories but "I'm-the-person-who" claims) sitting at the intersection of your expertise, the audience's obsessive pains, and untaken angles, with each pillar fanning into 5 differentiated angles. (3) High-converting carousels share a fixed anatomy — a scroll-stopping <10-word cover, slide-2 skepticism-reducer, one-idea body slides with open loops, and a save/share-triggering close — where saves come from reference-worthy frameworks and shares from "send this to the person who handles X." (4) Top operators systematize via locked master templates, hook swipe files, the five-beat story (Hook→Context→Discovery→Reward→CTA), and AI-for-mechanics/human-for-hook division. The deliverable is a concrete ICP→pillars→post concept→carousel-spec generation method an LLM can execute end to end.

findings.claim: Derive an ICP from public/scraped company data using 5 layers, not just firmographics
findings.detail: The complete ICP model has five dimensions, each with scrapable fields: (1) Firmographics — industry, employee count, revenue, geography, funding stage; (2) Technographics — CRM/tooling, adjacent tools, competitive solutions in their stack; (3) Behavioral signals — hiring patterns (job posts), funding events, technology migrations, content engagement; (4) Organizational readiness — presence of a decision-maker title, team size, process maturity (inferable from LinkedIn/org charts); (5) Negative indicators — churned verticals, underperforming sizes, red flags. Firmographics alone is insufficient: if won and lost deals share identical firmographics, the ICP lacks dimensionality and needs technographic/behavioral/organizational layers added.

findings.claim: The data-driven derivation process starts from closed-won deals and validates against closed-lost
findings.detail: Step 1: extract fields from last 12 months of closed business (industry, size, funding stage, tech stack, trigger event, decision-maker title, sales-cycle length). Step 2: sort by deal size + retention — highest-ACV/lowest-churn/fastest-expansion accounts reveal the ideal profile. Step 3: validate against closed-lost to ensure the criteria actually discriminate. Step 4: operationalize as an automated 0-100 score across all five layers. Step 5: review quarterly — teams refreshing ICP quarterly outperform annual refreshers by 20-35% on MQL-to-closed-won, and a clearly defined ICP correlates with ~68% higher win rates.

findings.claim: Layer JTBD onto the ICP — personas describe WHO, JTBD describes what they're trying to GET DONE
findings.detail: JTBD identifies what each stakeholder aims to achieve when buying, and pairs with personas rather than replacing them. Capture three job types per buyer: functional (the practical task — answer with demos/ROI calculators), emotional (how they want to feel — answer with testimonials/success stories), and social (how they want to be seen — answer with expert analyses/case studies). Method: mine the last 12-24 closed-won deals for recurring jobs-to-be-done, pains, triggers, objections, and the proof that moved them, then validate with 5-10 buyer interviews and sales-call notes.

findings.claim: For B2B, model the buying committee as 5 archetypes — each consumes different content
findings.detail: Five archetypes appear in every committee regardless of titles: Champion (internal advocate, usually a power-user — feed ROI + peer case studies); Economic Buyer (the single budget yes — feed financial impact, TCO, payback period, NOT features); Technical Evaluator (IT/eng — feed architecture + security docs, integrations, reliability); End User (cares about workflow fit/UX/time-to-value — feed demo videos + workflow content); Blocker/Skeptic (the most under-engaged role where most deals die — feed evidence not enthusiasm: security audit, retention data, failure-mode analysis). Supporting roles: compliance (SOC2/GDPR), legal, procurement. Practical implication: a single ICP yields multiple persona-content threads, one per role.

findings.claim: Content pillars are 3 claims of authority, not topic buckets
findings.detail: Discover 5-7 candidate pillars, narrow to 3 validated ones. A real pillar sits at the intersection of: (1) expertise that's genuinely yours, (2) problems the audience can't stop thinking about (actual, not assumed pain), and (3) angles nobody else covers (the 'name-removal test' — could five others write this identically?). Express each pillar as an 'I'm the person who...' statement (e.g., 'I reverse-engineer why specific B2B campaigns failed using actual numbers'), not a topic label like 'I post about marketing.' Maintain focus on the strongest pillar for ~4 weeks before adding others.

findings.claim: Map pains to pillars by scoring 'audience obsession' and classifying painkiller vs vitamin
findings.detail: Score each candidate angle 1-10 on audience obsession. Signal tiers: Tier 1 = DMs and detailed comments (the reader literally has this problem); Tier 4 = generic likes (lowest value). Pillar Health Threshold is 6+; kill anything below. Classify each angle as a painkiller (urgent need) or vitamin (nice-to-have) using the audience's own language, not creator jargon. Then for each pillar generate ~5 post concepts from distinct angles — Educator, Contrarian, Storyteller, Tactician, Vulnerable — to test signal diversity rather than chasing likes on one approach.

findings.claim: A 'Content DNA' document is the repeatable engine that turns a pillar into consistent posts
findings.detail: Content DNA components: Voice (3 adjectives + 'never sound like' constraints + 2-3 example posts), Pillars (the 'I'm-the-person-who' statements), Audience (who they are, their #1 problem, their language, what they've already tried), Constraints (platform, frequency, time), Winners Archive (top 5 posts + why they worked), Losers Archive (3 flops + why). Before any post ships, run five self-checks: Name-Removal Test, Specificity Audit (concrete numbers/stories/named examples), Hook Surgery (strongest possible opening), Delete Test (does every line earn its place), and Share-Trigger Check (one line worth screenshotting). One asset is then repurposed into 4 platform versions (LinkedIn, X, short-form, newsletter), not merely resized.

findings.claim: Carousel cover (hook) anatomy: <10 words, answers 'is this for me?' + 'what do I get?', visually incomplete
findings.detail: The cover is the entire shot at engagement — it must stop the scroll and earn the swipe, readable in under ~2 seconds (some sources cite a 0.7s read-time ceiling; never require zooming). Keep it under 8-10 words with one central idea. Proven cover formulas: 'Stop doing X. Do this instead', '3 signs you...', checklists, before/after splits, myth vs fact. Design tactic: make the cover feel incomplete (cropped diagram, partial checklist, blurred result) to force the swipe, plus a pattern interrupt (high-contrast color, expressive face, dramatic graphic) and a curiosity trigger.

findings.claim: Slide-by-slide structure: cover -> slide-2 skepticism-reducer -> one-idea body slides with open loops -> CTA close
findings.detail: Anatomy: Slide 1 = promise + specificity (hook). Slide 2 = reduce skepticism via 'why this matters' frame, roadmap, proof, or self-qualification question. Slides 3-9 = one point per slide, flashcard style, short punchy text + supporting visual, chained with open loops ('Next: the fix that takes 2 minutes'). Final slide = the ask, crystal-clear and aligned to the post goal. Put the CTA twice — one soft mid-carousel, one explicit on the last slide. Use fewer than ~12 words per slide. The narrative follows a five-beat story: Hook -> Context -> Discovery -> Reward -> CTA.

findings.claim: Saves and shares are different goals driven by different content — design for the one you want
findings.detail: Saves = 'people want to come back' — driven by checklists, step-by-steps, templates, decision frameworks; make the last slide reference-worthy (summary/cheat-sheet). An explicit 'save this' CTA on the final slide raises save rates ~25-40% vs none. Shares = content feels relevant to forward — frame it as 'share with the person who handles X' to give it social value. Rule of thumb: if the goal is reach, prioritize shares/sends; if the goal is authority/long-tail, prioritize saves. Carousels get ~1.9x the reach of single images and up to ~3x engagement; benchmark targets are 50%+ swipe-through and 5-10% save rate (adjust by niche).

findings.claim: The inform/entertain/prove value axis is operationalized through slide roles and engagement metrics
findings.detail: High-performers balance three jobs: inform (one digestible idea per slide), entertain (visual rhythm, pattern interrupts, expressive imagery to hold dwell time), and prove (named numbers, before/after, case data on the body/penultimate slide to earn the save). Track three metrics that map to these jobs: dwell time (entertain/hold), completion rate (inform/structure), and save+share rate (prove/value). 8-12 slides is the standard sweet spot for educational posts; 12-20 for deep guides/case studies; <4 slides barely beats a single image.

findings.claim: Top agencies/creators systematize carousels via locked templates, swipe files, and AI-for-mechanics
findings.detail: They build one master template per design system (colors, font hierarchy, spacing locked once, then duplicated per client to kill brand drift). They keep a reusable carousel library / swipe file of recurring problems and hook formulas (bold 5-8 word headline + pattern interrupt + curiosity trigger). They use a fixed slide pattern matched to funnel stage (5-beat: Hook->Context->Discovery->Reward->CTA). They A/B test by uploading the same body with different first-card hooks and letting the algorithm pick a winner within ~48 hours. Division of labor: AI handles mechanical parts (structure, first-draft copy, layout consistency, font systems); humans own the hook, the punchline, and the specific details.

recommendations: Adopt the 5-layer ICP model (firmographics, technographics, behavioral signals, organizational readiness, negative indicators) as the schema the scraper fills — and explicitly require a 'negative ICP' field so the pipeline can disqualify, not just qualify.

For B2B, expand a single ICP into a buying-committee matrix (5 archetypes x JTBD functional/emotional/social x proof-that-moves-them). Each cell becomes a content thread; this prevents the common failure of marketing only to the champion and ignoring the blocker.

Force pillars to pass the 'name-removal test' and be written as 'I'm-the-person-who' claims; reject any pillar scoring below 6 on the audience-obsession scale and tag every angle painkiller vs vitamin before drafting.
Generate exactly 5 angles per pillar from the 5 lenses (Educator, Contrarian, Storyteller, Tactician, Vulnerable) so the pipeline tests signal diversity instead of one safe take.

Lock a carousel spec template: cover <=10 words + pattern interrupt + incompleteness; slide-2 skepticism-reducer; one-idea body slides <=12 words with open loops; penultimate proof slide; final slide dual CTA (save-trigger + 'send to whoever handles X').

Decide saves-vs-shares per post up front and design accordingly — checklist/framework format for saves, 'tag the person who...' framing for shares — and instrument dwell time, completion rate, and save/share rate to close the loop.

Run the AI-for-mechanics / human-for-hook split: let the LLM produce structure + first-draft slide copy + 3-5 hook variants, then have a human pick/sharpen the hook and inject specific named numbers before it ships.
Maintain a Winners/Losers archive and a hook swipe file as persistent memory so the pipeline compounds — feed last week's completion/save data back into next week's concept selection.
risks: Most sources are vendor/agency marketing blogs (Landbase, TrueFuture, DesignLumo, Velocity Engine) with self-serving incentives; the quantitative claims (68% higher win rates, 25-40% save lift, 1.9x reach, 50% swipe-through, 5-10% save benchmark) are widely repeated but largely un-sourced to primary platform data and should be treated as directional, not gospel.

Engagement benchmarks vary enormously by niche, follower size, and B2B-vs-consumer; a B2B distributor account will not hit consumer-creator save/share rates, so absolute targets must be recalibrated per account from its own baseline.

Public/scraped company data captures firmographics and technographics well but is weak on real intent, JTBD, and buying-committee composition — the framework explicitly requires 5-10 buyer interviews + sales-call notes to validate, which a pure-scraping pipeline cannot supply; over-trusting scraped-only ICPs produces confident but wrong personas.

Instagram/Meta algorithm behavior and format performance shift frequently; 2025-2026 carousel reach advantages can erode (Meta periodically reprioritizes Reels), so the spec should be revalidated against live account analytics rather than assumed permanent.

Over-systematization (locked templates + AI first drafts) is exactly what produces generic, template-looking output the audience scrolls past; the human-owned hook + specific named numbers are the load-bearing differentiators and cannot be fully automated.

stream: Visual Reference Mining Methodology (Images & Carousels) — Global Best-in-Class + Local Competitors
summary: A repeatable visual-reference research method splits into two buckets: GLOBAL best-in-class (aspirational craft) and LOCAL competitors (market context). Discovery layers across Meta Ad Library + TikTok Creative Center (paid winners, with "started running" date as the #1 performance proxy), Instagram/LinkedIn organic carousels (ranked by a save/share-weighted engagement score), swipe-file tools like Foreplay.co (200M+ ad DB, Chrome-clip, carousel/format filters), and design galleries (Behance/Dribbble/Savee/Cosmos) for craft references. Programmatic collection runs through Apify actors (Facebook Ads Library Scraper at ~$0.65/1k ads; Instagram Scraper Pro for multi-profile engagement ranking) and Firecrawl schema-based extraction, ranked by a weighted engagement formula (saves 3pts, shares 4pts, comments 2pts, likes 0.5pts) plus run-duration for ads. The extraction step converts examples into a reusable "visual playbook" — carousel anatomy (hook/body/CTA), color triad, type pairing, layout grammar, hook taxonomy — encoded as design tokens + rules a generator (Higgsfield/Canva) can follow. The deliverable is a 5-stage reference-research module: Define → Discover → Collect → Extract → Encode.

findings.claim: Two buckets need different sources and different success metrics: GLOBAL = craft ceiling, LOCAL = market floor/context
findings.detail: GLOBAL best-in-class is best mined from design galleries (Behance for portfolio-quality case studies with rationale, Dribbble for trend/community polish, Savee for editorial mood boards, Cosmos as a discovery engine by category like typography/UI/graphic design) plus globally-curated ad winners (TikTok Creative Center surfaces 'top-performing ads globally' via TikTok's own algorithm; Meta Ad Library for any brand running paid). LOCAL competitors are mined by advertiser-name search in Meta Ad Library + handle search on IG/LinkedIn + local hashtag/geo filters. Rank GLOBAL refs by craft/recognition; rank LOCAL refs by measured engagement and ad run-duration. Keep them in separate boards so aspirational craft never gets diluted by mediocre local norms.

findings.claim: Meta Ad Library 'started running' date is the single most useful free proxy for ad performance
findings.detail: Workflow: open facebook.com/ads/library, enter exact advertiser name, enable ALL formats, remove date filters, set country (required). Winning-creative criteria: (1) run duration >=30 days (shorter = a test, not a scaled winner; 60-90+ days = almost certainly profitable since advertisers don't keep paying for losers), (2) variation density >=3 visible variants (serious investment), (3) multiple long-duration ads from same advertiser ('one winner is chance; five is a system'), (4) pattern replicable in your brand voice. Sort by start date (oldest first) to surface long-runners. This is the closest thing to a free 'this creative converts' signal.

findings.claim: TikTok Creative Center pre-filters by performance but is curated, not exhaustive; cross-reference with the Ads Library by handle
findings.detail: Creative Center (ads.tiktok.com/business/creativecenter) lets you filter by industry, region, objective, format, like count, CTR rank, conversion rank, and date, and exposes engagement stats + directional CTR/conversion rank — but you only see what TikTok chooses to surface. For direct competitor coverage, supplement with TikTok's searchable Ads Library (by advertiser name) since brands missing from Creative Center still appear there. Neither TikTok nor Meta offers comprehensive programmatic creative access with simple auth — that gap is why third-party scrapers exist.

findings.claim: Foreplay.co is the category swipe-file tool: 200M+ ad discovery DB, Chrome clipper, carousel/format filtering, natural-language search
findings.detail: Save ads in one click via Chrome extension from Facebook Ad Library, TikTok Creative Center, TikTok Top Ads, and LinkedIn into curated boards ('Swipe Files'). Discovery DB covers 200M+ ads across FB/IG/TikTok/YouTube/LinkedIn; filter by niche, FORMAT (video, image, CAROUSEL, DCO), language, B2B/B2C, running duration, and date. Each ad carries creative, copy, CTA, landing-page URL, live status, and run-duration. Auto-captures desktop+mobile landing-page screenshots and supports natural-language text search across your whole file. This is the manual-curation layer that complements programmatic scraping.

findings.claim: Programmatic competitor collection runs on Apify actors; Firecrawl handles schema-based extraction from arbitrary URLs
findings.detail: Meta: Facebook/Meta Ad Library scrapers on Apify extract creatives, copy, images/videos, run-status, advertiser/page transparency for FB/IG/Messenger/Audience Network — pricing from ~$0.005/ad (Graph API-backed) to ~$0.65/1k ads (bulk). Instagram organic: Instagram Post Scraper (~$1/1k) and Instagram Scraper Pro return per-post likesCount, commentsCount, videoViewCount, contentType (photo/video/CAROUSEL/reel), timestamp, displayUrl, caption, hashtags; Scraper Pro takes comma-separated handles in a 'competitors' mode and outputs engagementRates[], followerGrowth[], contentStrategy[], marketPositioning[]. Firecrawl /scrape accepts a JSON schema and returns typed structured data (numbers as numbers) resilient to HTML changes — good for one-off profile/gallery pages, while Apify is better for IG at scale. Note: Meta has NO public creative API (Marketing API is gated to your own ad accounts), so scraping the web UI is required.

findings.claim: Rank collected posts with a SAVE/SHARE-weighted engagement score, not raw likes — and weight ad winners by run-duration
findings.detail: Base rate = (Likes + Comments + Saves + Shares) / Followers x 100. But the algorithm weights are unequal: SAVES are the strongest signal (one save outweighs dozens of likes; highest weight for Explore placement), SHARES next (off-network distribution = quality signal), COMMENTS (deliberate effort), LIKES weakest. Practical ranking score: saves x3, shares x4, comments x2, likes x0.5 (then normalize per-follower or per-reach). For carousels, add a completion/dwell proxy (saves + comments correlate with full swipe-through). For paid ads where engagement isn't visible, substitute run-duration + variation-density as the rank signal. Also weight early-hour engagement since first-hour velocity drives distribution.

findings.claim: Carousel anatomy is a reusable grammar: Hook/Cover -> Body (one idea/slide) -> CTA, with hooks following 6 psychological patterns
findings.detail: COVER/HOOK: bold headline 5-8 words (largest text on slide) + visual pattern-interrupt (high-contrast color, expressive face, dramatic graphic that stops scroll in <50ms) + curiosity trigger (partial reveal / surprising stat / open question) + directional cue (subtle arrow inviting first swipe). 'Don't write a title slide — write a reason to swipe.' BODY: one idea per slide, lead with the point then elaborate, vary rhythm (asymmetric beats uniform), scannable for mobile, transition phrases for momentum. CTA/FINAL: match action to content (Save=frameworks/checklists, Share=team-useful, Comment=opinion, Click=deeper resource). Hook taxonomy = 6 patterns activating curiosity, FOMO, greed, defensiveness, relief, or disbelief. Optimal length: IG 8-10 slides (highest engagement); LinkedIn 7-10 (best depth/completion tradeoff); 1080x1350 (4:5) for max feed real estate.

findings.claim: The reference-to-playbook step is concrete: collapse examples into design tokens (color/type/layout) + rules a generator can follow
findings.detail: Color: extract ONE primary + neutral base + ONE accent (accent reserved for emphasis, no competing colors); tools like Peel Studio run Claude/Gemini vision to extract every color with semantic role (primary/background/accent) weighted by visual prominence. Type: one display + one body font; vision extraction returns family/weight/size/line-height organized by usage (heading/body/caption). Layout grammar by content type: educational=clean grid + strong hierarchy + minimal decoration; POV story=editorial, large headlines; product=structured cards/labels/screenshots; data=bold stat treatment + restrained palette. Hierarchy rule: primary (headline) dominates, secondary supports, tertiary (logo/page number/icon) stays stable; negative space separates ideas. Encode all of this as JSON design tokens + a prompt-rules block (style direction, palette hex, font pairing, slide-template per type, hook pattern) that feeds an image generator — turning 'steal the structure, not the words' into machine-followable specs.

findings.claim: Build the swipe file by screenshotting first slides until patterns repeat, then templatize — 30 saves reveals the recurring structure
findings.detail: Manual heuristic that scales: every time a post stops your scroll, screenshot slide 1; after ~30 you see the same patterns repeat; steal the structure (layout, hook type, slide flow), not the words. Analysis of 200M+ reaching posts shows the top 1% of carousels share identifiable design principles. This is the human-judgment complement to programmatic ranking — use it to label/tag references with the playbook fields (hook pattern, layout type, color triad) so the dataset becomes queryable design knowledge rather than a pile of screenshots.

recommendations: Adopt the 5-stage reference-research MODULE. (1) DEFINE: pick the job (job-to-be-done, platform, format=image|carousel, GLOBAL vs LOCAL bucket, success metric). (2) DISCOVER: GLOBAL via Behance/Dribbble/Savee/Cosmos + TikTok Creative Center + Foreplay 200M DB; LOCAL via Meta Ad Library (advertiser search) + IG/LinkedIn handle & geo-hashtag search. (3) COLLECT: Foreplay Chrome-clip for manual finds; Apify (Facebook Ads Library Scraper + Instagram Scraper Pro) for bulk; Firecrawl schema-scrape for arbitrary pages. (4) EXTRACT: rank by weighted engagement (saves x3, shares x4, comments x2, likes x0.5; ads by run-duration>=30d + variants>=3); run vision extraction (Peel/Claude vision) on top N to pull color/type/layout/hook. (5) ENCODE: write a versioned visual-playbook = JSON design tokens + prompt-rules block.

Inputs spec for the module: { job, platform[], format(image|carousel), bucket(global|local), competitor_handles[], advertiser_names[], hashtags[], geo, top_n, refresh_cadence }. Outputs spec: a tagged reference dataset (creative URL, format, metrics, run-duration, bucket), a ranked shortlist, and the visual-playbook artifact (palette hex triad, font pairing, slide templates per content-type, hook taxonomy, layout grammar) that Higgsfield/Canva/Lovable can consume directly.

Use the right tool per layer: Meta Ad Library (free, deepest paid coverage, run-duration signal) + TikTok Creative Center (free, algorithm-curated winners) for paid; Foreplay.co for cross-platform manual swipe + format filtering; Apify for programmatic IG/FB at scale; Firecrawl for typed schema extraction of one-off pages; Behance/Dribbble/Savee/Cosmos strictly for GLOBAL craft references; Peel Studio or a Claude-vision pass for token extraction.

Encode the playbook as enforceable rules, not vibes: ONE primary + neutral + ONE accent color; one display + one body font; per-content-type slide template (educational/POV/product/data); cover-slide formula (5-8 word headline + pattern interrupt + curiosity trigger + directional cue); 8-10 slides IG / 7-10 LinkedIn; 1080x1350. These map cleanly to generator prompts and prevent template-default output.

For the operator B2B context specifically: run LOCAL bucket on BR distributor/commerce competitors via Apify IG Scraper Pro (handle list) + Meta Ad Library BR; run GLOBAL bucket on best-in-class B2B carousels (CJHub/Vinci/Faire/Abacatepay-style) and store as separate boards. Feed the extracted playbook tokens into the existing Higgsfield editorial cover patterns (typography hero cream / concept diagram dark) so refs become reproducible templates, not one-offs.

Refresh on a cadence (weekly for LOCAL competitors, monthly for GLOBAL craft) and re-rank; archive stale refs. Persist the playbook + dataset to the vault (3-Resources) so it compounds across content sessions instead of being re-derived each time.
risks: Meta has NO public creative API (Marketing API gated to your own ad accounts) and TikTok transparency has major gaps — both require web-UI scraping, which can break and may sit in ToS gray areas; use Apify actors that handle this and monitor for breakage.

Engagement metrics from scrapers are public-surface only: SAVES and SHARES (the strongest algorithmic signals) and REACH are NOT exposed publicly, so any organic ranking is an approximation built from likes+comments+views. State this assumption explicitly and treat rankings as directional.

'Top performing' for ads is inferred from run-duration/variation density, not actual ROAS — a long-running ad is a strong but not certain winner; avoid over-fitting the playbook to a single advertiser's bias.
Vision-based token extraction (Peel/Claude) can mislabel semantic color roles or miss brand-specific nuance; human review of the extracted playbook is required before it drives generation.

Copying structure is fine; copying specific creative/copy/IP is not — keep the rule 'steal the structure, not the words/assets' to avoid brand and legal exposure, especially when scraping competitor visuals.
Mixing GLOBAL craft refs and mediocre LOCAL norms in one board degrades the aspirational ceiling; enforce bucket separation or the generator will regress to local-average output.

stream: Productizing an AI "company → ICP-targeted visual content" engine: wedge, pricing, packaging, Brazil/LATAM angle, and 0→1 GTM
summary: The market for AI visual-content generation is crowded at the "make pretty posts" layer (Predis.ai, Ocoya, AdCreative.ai, Creatopy) but thin at the layer this product actually occupies: ingesting a company's identity + ICP and producing on-brand, audience-targeted visual assets. The defensible wedge is NOT "another social tool for everyone" — it is B2B-focused operators who need ICP-specific creative at volume: marketing agencies (highest willingness to pay, per-client billing, whitelabel demand) as the revenue wedge, and in-house B2B/social marketers as the product-fit wedge. The winning model in this category is hybrid: a per-brand-workspace base fee plus usage credits, with agency multi-brand tiers as the margin engine. Brazil/LATAM is a genuine structural advantage for a BR-based operator: fastest-growing SaaS region (~23% in 2024, market doubling to ~$46B by 2027), strong preference for Portuguese-localized product (68% of BR buyers prefer native-language UI/training), near-zero-fee Pix Automático recurring rails, and almost no incumbent shipping a Portuguese-native ICP-content engine. Recommendation: wedge on B2B agencies + in-house B2B marketers, price per-brand-workspace + credits, ship a reverse-trial (full features for 7-14 days, then drop to a capped free tier), and make the "aha" the moment a user sees a full on-brand, ICP-targeted asset set generated from just a URL + ICP description in under 5 minutes.

findings.claim: The category splits into two layers — and the product's real competition is the upper layer, where pricing is much higher.
findings.detail: Lower layer (generic social posts): Predis.ai ($19/$40/$212 mo for 1 / up-to-4 / unlimited brands), Ocoya ($15-$159), Pressmaster ($12 starter). Upper layer (ad/brand-grade creative): AdCreative.ai charges $39 Starter / $249 Professional / $599 Ultimate / custom Enterprise (annual ~40% off → $25/$149/$359). An ICP-targeted engine sits in the upper layer (brand-grade, conversion-oriented) so it can anchor pricing to AdCreative/Jasper, not to $19 social schedulers.

findings.claim: Per-brand-workspace is the dominant structural unit in this category, and it scales naturally into agency tiers.
findings.detail: Predis.ai gates explicitly on brand count: 1 brand (Core $19), up to 4 brands (Rise $40), unlimited brands (Enterprise+ $212), plus social-account caps (10/20/60) and credits (1,300/3,200/10,000 = ~65/160/500 AI images). This 'brand workspace = pricing axis' pattern is exactly right for an ICP engine because each ICP/brand is a distinct knowledge object. Agencies pay for more brands; in-house teams pay for more credits.

findings.claim: Credit/usage models are now the category default and are growing fastest, but pure usage pricing alone is risky.
findings.detail: 85% of companies use some usage-based pricing; credit-based models grew 126% YoY. Adobe's reference model: free 1,000 generative credits, then capacity-capped tiers $10-$200 for 2,000-50,000 credits. Metronome warns cost-plus credit models break when underlying inference costs swing. Bessemer/Reforge consensus for 2025-26 is HYBRID: predictable per-seat or per-workspace base + flexible credits for variable AI workload.

findings.claim: Agencies are the highest-willingness-to-pay wedge: they already resell AI content at 10-50x the tool's cost.
findings.detail: Agencies package AI content services at $1,500-$5,000/mo (boutique/SMB), $5,000-$10,000/mo (mid-market), $10,000-$50,000+/mo (enterprise), and whitelabel platforms run at 30-70% margins. An agency paying $200-$500/mo for a multi-brand ICP engine and billing clients $2K-$5K/mo has obvious ROI — this is why agencies are the revenue wedge, even though in-house B2B marketers may be the cleaner product-fit wedge.

findings.claim: The single biggest GTM lever is reaching the activation 'aha' fast — and for content tools that means 'generated on-brand asset from a URL' in minutes.
findings.detail: Activation is the strongest predictor of conversion; every 10-minute delay to first value costs ~8% in conversion. Free trials convert at 15-25% vs 2-5% for freemium. Best-in-class onboarding (cited in the category) scrapes the company website, builds a strategy brief on an AI canvas, and produces the first content piece automatically — i.e., the URL-to-asset flow IS the aha. For this product the aha is: paste URL + describe ICP → see a complete, on-brand, ICP-targeted asset set in <5 min, no manual brand setup.

findings.claim: Reverse trial (hybrid) is the fastest-growing packaging model and the best fit here — it beats pure freemium and pure trial.
findings.detail: Hybrid freemium+premium-trial is used by ~65% of PLG SaaS in 2026 and often outperforms either model alone: low-friction permanent entry PLUS a full-featured time-limited window so users feel the loss of premium when it ends. Practical design: 7-14 day full access (all ICP intelligence, all formats, exports), then auto-downgrade to a capped free tier (e.g., 1 brand, watermarked exports, low credits). Gate VALUE AMPLIFIERS (multi-brand, whitelabel, high-res/un-watermarked export, brand-voice memory, bulk/batch, competitor analysis), never the core aha.

findings.claim: Brazil/LATAM is a structural advantage, not just a home-market convenience.
findings.detail: LATAM SaaS is the fastest-growing region globally (~23% in 2024 vs ~17% global), market doubling from ~$22B (2023) to ~$46B (2027), with Brazil the highest projected CAGR 2025-30 and the largest software market in the region. SMBs digitize faster than enterprises. Critically, 68% of Brazilian software buyers prefer training/UI in Portuguese and value localized, region-specific features — and almost no ICP-content engine ships Portuguese-native. A BR operator with an existing a live B2B use case can win a category that US incumbents under-serve.

findings.claim: Brazilian pricing requires PPP adjustment + local payment rails, and Pix Automático makes recurring SaaS newly viable with near-zero fees.
findings.detail: Brazil is PPP Tier-2 (Middle Income) needing ~30-50% discounts vs US prices; adoption accelerates when prices drop 40-60% to match local purchasing power. Pix Automático (launched June 2025) enables consented recurring charges at near-zero fee vs 4-7% card fees in Brazil — directly protecting margin on low-ticket plans. Offering local rails (Pix) can lift conversion ~30%. So: a US price of $49 should become roughly R$99-R$149/mo on Pix, billed via Pix Automático.

findings.claim: The 0→1 GTM that works for a solo/lean founder in this category is build-in-public + founder-led content + product-as-distribution, not paid ads.
findings.detail: Lovable hit $200M ARR in 12 months largely via CEO building in public and seconds-to-value PLG; the AI-native pattern is to engineer distribution into the product (users become distributors) and run positioning/content/outreach through an AI stack. For this product: the engine itself produces the marketing proof (dogfood — generate your brand's content publicly), every exported asset can carry an attribution/footer on free tier, and the founder ships ICP-content case studies as content. Beachhead = one sharp ICP (e.g., BR B2B distributors/commerce B2B vertical) before expanding.

findings.claim: The differentiator vs every generic tool is the 'company + ICP → targeted creative' intelligence layer — that must be the product's spine, not a feature.
findings.detail: Category leaders already do ICP definition (behavioral/psychographic clusters) feeding a strategy builder that auto-generates positioning, messaging pillars, and channel-targeted assets that update in real time. Generic tools (Predis/Ocoya) generate pretty posts but are NOT ICP-aware; AdCreative is conversion-aware but not deeply ICP/brand-knowledge-aware. The wedge is owning the loop: ingest company (URL/brand kit) → model ICP → generate visual assets tuned to that ICP's tone/format/channel → learn from performance.

recommendations: WEDGE: Lead with B2B marketing agencies (and fractional/solo social media managers serving B2B) as the revenue wedge, with in-house B2B/social marketers as the product-fit wedge. Reason: agencies have the highest willingness to pay (they resell content at $1.5K-$50K/mo), need multi-brand + whitelabel, and validate the per-brand pricing axis. SMBs/solopreneurs are too price-sensitive and churny to anchor 0→1. Pick ONE sharp beachhead ICP first — e.g., BR B2B commerce/distribution (the BR B2B vertical) — prove it, then widen.

PRICING (hybrid: per-brand-workspace base + usage credits). Suggested global ladder: Free (1 brand, ~50 credits/mo, watermarked exports) → Solo/Pro ~$39-$49/mo (1-2 brands, ~150-200 credits, un-watermarked, brand-voice memory) → Agency ~$199-$299/mo (up to ~10 brands, ~1,000 credits, whitelabel/client folders, bulk generation) → Agency Scale/Enterprise custom (unlimited brands, API, seats, dedicated). Anchor against AdCreative ($39/$249/$599) and Jasper ($39 Creator), NOT against $19 social schedulers. Sell credit top-ups (e.g., +1,200 credits / ~$29) like Predis.

PACKAGING: Use a reverse trial — 7-14 days of full access (all ICP intelligence, all formats, un-watermarked export, all brands) that auto-downgrades to the capped free tier. Gate value amplifiers only: multi-brand workspaces, whitelabel/remove-branding, high-res & un-watermarked export, persistent brand-voice/ICP memory, batch/bulk generation, competitor analysis, API. NEVER gate the core aha (URL+ICP → first on-brand asset set).

ACTIVATION: Engineer the aha to fire in under 5 minutes — onboarding scrapes the company URL, infers brand kit + a draft ICP, and auto-generates a full first asset set (carousel + captions + ad variants) the user can immediately export. Instrument 'time-to-first-exported-asset' as the north-star activation metric; every minute of delay costs ~8% conversion.

BRAZIL/LATAM: Ship Portuguese-native UI, onboarding, and prompts from day one (68% of BR buyers prefer native-language product). Price in BRL with ~40-50% PPP adjustment (e.g., global $49 → ~R$99-R$149/mo) and bill via Pix Automático to capture near-zero-fee recurring revenue and ~30% conversion lift. Position as 'the only ICP-content engine that thinks in Brazilian-Portuguese B2B' — a category US incumbents do not serve.

MINIMUM LOVABLE PRODUCT to charge for: (1) URL/brand-kit ingest → auto brand profile; (2) ICP definition (guided + editable); (3) generation of a coherent multi-asset set tuned to that ICP (carousel + captions + 2-3 ad/visual variants) in the brand's voice; (4) export (gated quality/watermark by tier); (5) per-brand workspace + credit metering; (6) Pix/Stripe billing. Defer: scheduling, analytics dashboards, deep competitor scraping, video — add after the content-generation loop is proven and paid for.

0→1 GTM: Run founder-led build-in-public using the engine on yourself — publicly generate and ship your brand's ICP-targeted content as ongoing proof. Put a subtle 'made with [product]' footer on free-tier exports so every shared asset is a distribution loop. Land the first 10-20 paying agencies/operators via direct outreach in the beachhead vertical (warm BR B2B network), turn them into case studies, then open self-serve. Avoid paid acquisition until activation→paid conversion is validated.

DEFENSIBILITY: Make the ICP + brand-knowledge memory the moat — the longer a customer's brand/ICP lives in the system and learns from outputs, the higher the switching cost. Generic post generators (Predis/Ocoya) can copy a feature but not your accumulated per-brand ICP knowledge graph. Treat 'company+ICP intelligence' as the spine, visual generation as the surface.
risks: Category is crowded and price-anchored low — if you position as 'another social content tool' you get compared to Predis at $19 and lose. The ICP/B2B-brand-grade framing is essential to escape the commodity floor.

Pure usage/credit pricing exposes you to volatile model-inference costs (Metronome's warning); the per-brand base fee must cover fixed cost so credits are pure variable margin. Watch gross margin per generated asset closely.

Agencies demand whitelabel + multi-seat + API early; under-delivering on these stalls the revenue wedge. But over-building them before product-market fit burns runway — sequence them right after first paying agencies, not before.
PPP/BRL pricing creates arbitrage and support-cost asymmetry; geo-gate Pix/BRL pricing to BR and prevent global customers from buying the cheap BR tier (IP/billing-country checks).
Brazilian B2B SMBs are price-sensitive and can churn fast; the reverse-trial must convert on demonstrated ROI (asset volume saved, agency client billings) not novelty, or free-tier costs eat margin.

Founder-led/build-in-public GTM concentrates risk on the operator's time and audience; if the beachhead ICP is too narrow (single the operator brand-like vertical) TAM may be small — validate expansion adjacency early.
AdCreative.ai, Jasper, and m1-project-style ICP tools could add the missing piece and out-resource a solo founder; speed-to-localized-BR-niche and ICP-memory lock-in are the only durable defenses.
